summary: DeVault walks through building a high-performance finger server using Linux's io_uring async I/O interface, written in his then-unreleased programming language (Hare). He uses the deliberately simple and obsolete Finger protocol as a test bed for designing an io_uring wrapper, explaining the ring buffer architecture, submission/completion queue model, and how it avoids costly syscalls. The post doubles as a tutorial on both io_uring concepts and Hare's language features including error handling, tagged unions, slices, and state machines. He implements a complete client state machine handling query parsing, plan file reading, CRLF conversion, and error forwarding, all driven by async I/O events.
key_points: io_uring solves Linux's long-standing async I/O problem by using shared memory ring buffers between kernel and userspace, avoiding syscall overhead
The Finger protocol's simplicity makes it an ideal test case for exercising a new async I/O interface without protocol complexity getting in the way
The server uses a carefully planned state machine with five states, where each state transition corresponds to an io_uring I/O completion event
Memory is pre-planned so that buffer sizes account for worst-case CRLF expansion, enabling static insert without reallocation
The language's error handling requires explicit action on every error via tagged unions, with propagation (?) and assertion (!) operators
money_quotes: After being designed in the 70's and then completely forgotten about for 50 years, it's the perfect small and simple network protocol to test drive this new interface with.
It more or less solves the async I/O problem for Linux, which Linux (and Unix at large) has struggled to do for a long time.
I went for a backlog of 256 connections (overkill for a finger server, but hey), and set reuseport so you can achieve CLOUD SCALE by running several daemons at once.
In this language, we require the user to explicitly do something about errors.
The somewhat provocative name allegedly comes from an older usage of the word to mean 'a snitch' or a member of the FBI.
And the code is GPL'd, but not AGPL'd, so you aren't entitled to it if you finger me!
key_insight: The deliberately obsolete Finger protocol serves as a perfect minimal test case for exploring io_uring's async I/O model and showcasing a new systems language's design choices around error handling, memory management, and state machines.
